html:
Studio Alice is not the ¥550, 0.3x book, 9% yield setup in the prior draft. That was stale. The current stock is ¥1,762, market cap about ¥30.3 billion, 0.99x book, 23.9x company-guided FY2/2027 EPS, and a 2.8% dividend yield. On headline P/E it is not optically cheap.

The thesis is subtler and better than that: the equity is being priced like a low-ROE, ex-growth Japanese children’s-photo chain, while the enterprise value is only about ¥13 billion after net cash and the business is still capable of producing ¥2.5-3.0 billion of annual operating profit and ¥2.5 billion-plus of pre-dividend free cash flow if the recent price/mix shift holds.

The market is right about the main problem. Japan’s births fell again in 2025 to about 705,809, a record low and down 2.1% year over year. Studio Alice’s core demand pool is shrinking. FY2/2026 sales fell 7.5% to ¥32.9 billion, operating profit fell 30.6% to ¥2.10 billion, and the photography segment’s profit fell 25.4%. The business is not a secular compounder.

But the market is likely too linear in how it extrapolates that decline. Studio Alice is not selling generic prints to a casual customer base. It owns a ritualized purchase occasion: Shichi-Go-San, baby ceremonies, birthdays, coming-of-age photos, kimono rental, and family milestone photography. The customer count can decline while ticket size rises, and the April 2026 monthly data is the key new evidence. March-April FY2/2027 sales were down only 3.0% cumulatively despite customer counts down meaningfully, because average spend rose sharply. In April alone, all-store customer count was 90.8% of last year, but average spend was 109.3%; existing-store customer count was 92.4%, with spend at 109.2%. That is not a dead brand. That is a shrinking-volume business still taking price and mix.

The company is also not standing still. Management is closing and consolidating weaker studios, pushing labor productivity, expanding on-location photography, testing new formats, selling image-data-only plans, growing adult ceremony/kimonos through “Furiho,” and trying to increase baby and maternity usage. At FY2/2026 year-end it still had 407 children’s-photo studios, 398 direct and 9 franchise, after 7 openings and 14 closures. That is controlled pruning, not a collapse.

The balance sheet matters, but not in the simplistic “negative EV equals moonshot” way. Cash and deposits were ¥18.3 billion at February 2026, cash equivalents ¥17.84 billion, against less than ¥1.0 billion of lease debt. Net cash is roughly ¥16.9-17.4 billion, or about ¥995-1,020 per share. With the stock at ¥1,762, investors are paying roughly ¥740-770 per share for the operating business after net cash. That means the operating business is valued at only about 5.3x FY2/2027 guided operating profit and around 5x normalized free cash flow before dividends if OCF stays near ¥4.8-5.0 billion and capex near ¥2.3-2.5 billion.

The headline P/E looks expensive because the cash is idle and ROE is low. The enterprise valuation looks cheap because the operating business is not actually impaired enough to deserve a runoff multiple. The variant perception is that Studio Alice does not need growth. It only needs revenue to decline slowly while price/mix, store pruning, and labor productivity keep operating profit in the ¥2.4-3.0 billion range. In that scenario, the stock is a cash-rich, underlevered domestic service annuity trading near book, not a melting cube.

The April 2026 monthly report is especially important because it supports management’s FY2/2027 guidance: sales ¥33.6 billion, operating profit ¥2.45 billion, net income ¥1.25 billion, EPS ¥73.60, and a maintained ¥50 dividend. The guide implies only 2.0% sales growth but 16.9% operating profit growth. If the first two months were only down 3.0% while spend per customer was up high single digits, the earnings guide is credible, and possibly conservative if autumn Shichi-Go-San demand normalizes.

There is also a strategic angle the market largely ignores. Fuji Film owns 20.31% of voting rights and has a director connection tied to a business alliance. Toransu Japan owns 23.36%. This ownership structure reduces float and may limit activist pressure, but it also means a large imaging company has a meaningful strategic seat next to a national photo-studio chain with data, brand, customer relationships, and physical family-photo infrastructure. A full acquisition is not the base case, but the downside case of an abandoned orphan is less credible when a strategic imaging shareholder remains this involved.

Retail positioning is not euphoric. Yahoo Finance shows message-board sentiment at 100% “wait and see,” while margin data shows more short balance than long balance. This is not a crowded bull story. It is a small Standard-market name, covered mostly as a dividend/yutai stock, with investors focused on falling births and weak sales rather than enterprise value, cash conversion, and the price/mix inflection.

The biggest risk is that April’s price/mix is temporary. If customers keep falling 8-10% and average spend fades back to low single digits, FY2/2027 guidance will miss, the business will drift toward ¥1.5 billion operating profit, and the market will stop giving full credit for book value. Another risk is capital allocation. Cash is large, but management is not aggressively buying back shares. The ¥50 dividend is covered, but it is not enough by itself to force a rerating. This is why the thesis needs operating proof, not just a cash screen.

My base case is that Studio Alice earns about ¥75-85 EPS in FY2/2027 and moves toward ¥90-100 EPS in FY2/2028 as store pruning and spend-per-customer gains offset demographics. Free cash flow before dividends stays around ¥2.5-3.0 billion. A fair value of 1.15-1.25x book, or about 14-16x EPS plus net cash support, puts the stock around ¥2,200-2,300.

The bull case is not demographic growth. It is margin recapture. If revenue stabilizes around ¥33-34 billion and operating margin returns to 8-9%, operating profit becomes ¥2.7-3.1 billion, EPS moves toward ¥95-115, and the market starts valuing the company as a cash-rich, dominant niche service franchise rather than a declining photo retailer. Add even a modest buyback or clearer Fuji-linked strategic optionality, and 1.5x book is achievable. That gives a bull target of ¥2,700.

Bear case: FY2/2027 revenue misses, customer count keeps falling near 10%, price/mix breaks, and operating profit falls toward ¥1.5 billion. The market gives only 0.75x book despite the cash because ROE is structurally sub-3%. Bear target: ¥1,350, or 23% downside.

Base case: guidance is met or modestly beaten, April’s high-single-digit spend lift proves durable enough, operating profit recovers to ¥2.45-2.7 billion, and the stock rerates to 1.25x book. Base target: ¥2,250, or 28% upside from ¥1,762.

Bull case: Studio consolidation plus price/mix creates ¥3.0 billion-plus operating profit, EPS exceeds ¥100, and capital allocation improves through a buyback, dividend raise, or strategic action involving the Fuji relationship. Bull target: ¥2,700, or 53% upside.
